name: Dawāsir Arabic (Dammam, Eastern Province)

ancestors.notes: Shares the Eastern-Province Gulf overlay (qaf→[ɡ], *k→[tʃ] kaškaša, *ɡ→[dʒ] affrication, aw/ay→oː/eː, /ɑː/→[ɒː] rounding) with the sedentary Sharqiyya leaf, but sits on a Najdi-Bedouin substrate, so parenting is partial.

notes: STUB. The Arabic of the Dawāsir tribe (بنو دواسر) as spoken in urban Dammam, Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia. The Dawāsir are a Sunni, Najdi/southern-origin tribe who migrated via Bahrain and settled the Eastern coast; typologically their dialect is a Najdi-Bedouin core with a Gulf overlay (Alaodini 2019). Modeled DELTA vs the sedentary ar-SA-x-sharqiyya leaf: jīm ج → [j] (yōd) as the PRIMARY (traditional/local) reflex — the distinctive Dawāsir marker, acquired under Bahraini Sunni-ʕArab prestige contact — with the affricate [dʒ] kept as the secondary, innovative and now-dominant reflex (Alaodini 2019 reports [dʒ] at 76%, [j] receding toward the Najdi/Riyadh koiné, led by men and the young; p.94, p.125). This is the mirror image of the sedentary Baḥārna/Hasawi leaf, which has [dʒ] primary. NAJDI-BEDOUIN substrate features documented for the variety but NOT yet modeled at stub tier (needs a research-tier pass): retained interdentals ث/ذ/ظ with *dˤ→ðˤ merger (Alaodini p.42); the gahawa syndrome, epenthetic a after gutturals (p.46); *k→[tʃ] kaškaša in stem and 2fsg suffix and *ɡ(<*q)→[dʒ] affrication before front vowels, but NEVER the interior-Najdi [ts]/[dz] type (p.43-45); Najdi short-vowel/syllable rules with no labial i>u raising, explicitly unlike Bahrani (p.54); word-medial long /ɑː/→[ɒː] backing/rounding, a pan-Eastern feature shared with al-Hasa and Bahrain (p.52, p.83); aw/ay→oː/eː monophthongization with residual word-final diphthongs (p.51-55). INPUT CONTRACT: transcribes orthography+harakat; short vowels surface only when the input is vocalised. Inherits the Sharqiyya/Gulf stress cascade.

sources.author: Alaodini, H.
sources.title: A Sociolinguistic Study of the Dawāsir Dialect in Dammam, Eastern Arabia: Variation and Change in a Tribal Community (PhD thesis)
sources.notes: The primary description of Dammam Dawāsir. Documents jīm [j]~[dʒ] variation (traditional [j], innovative [dʒ] 76%), the Najdi-Bedouin substrate (interdentals, gahawa, kaškaša, Najdi vowels) and the Gulf overlay ([ɒː] rounding, monophthongization), and the change in progress toward the Najdi/Riyadh koiné.

sources.author: Ingham, B.
sources.title: North East Arabian Dialects
sources.publisher: Kegan Paul International (Library of Arabic Linguistics, Monograph 3)
sources.notes: Groups the Eastern-Province bedouin ʕAjmān and Dawāsir as a 'Southern Najdi' bedouin type, defined negatively by the ABSENCE of *k→[tʃ] / *ɡ→[dz] affrication and by southern markers (retained ɡ in abgi, da 'this', jayy) — the bedouin pole against which the urban, affricating Dammam Dawāsir of Alaodini can be read as Gulf-influenced.

sources.author: Johnstone, T.M.
sources.title: Eastern Arabian Dialect Studies
sources.notes: Johnstone's Dōsiri texts (mixed ʿAjmān-Dawāsir) are the classical description; source of the *ǧīm→[j] lenition attribution for the tribe.

location.notes: Dammam, Eastern Province, Saudi Arabia — the urban seat of the Dawāsir community studied by Alaodini (2019).
